(or **electricity network**) is an interconnected network for [electricity delivery](/wiki/Electricity_delivery "Electricity delivery") from producers to consumers. Electrical grids consist of [power stations](/wiki/Power_station "Power station"), [electrical substations](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation") to step [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") up or down, [electric power transmission](/wiki/High_voltage_transmission_line "High voltage transmission line") to carry power over long distances, and finally [electric power distribution](/wiki/Electric_power_distribution "Electric power distribution") to customers. In that last step, voltage is stepped down again to the required service voltage. Power stations are typically built close to energy sources and far from densely populated areas. Electrical grids vary in size and can cover whole countries or continents. From small to large there are [microgrids](/wiki/Microgrid "Microgrid"), [wide area synchronous grids](/wiki/Wide_area_synchronous_grid "Wide area synchronous grid"), and [super grids](/wiki/Super_grid "Super grid"). The combined transmission and distribution network is part of electricity delivery, known as the *power grid*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Electric grid](/w/index.php?title=Electric_grid&redirect=no "Electric grid"))

Interconnected network for delivering electricity to consumers

For other uses, see [Grid (disambiguation)](/wiki/Grid_(disambiguation) "Grid (disambiguation)").

"Power grid" redirects here. For the board game, see [Power Grid](/wiki/Power_Grid "Power Grid").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Electricity_grid_simple-_North_America.svg/500px-Electricity_grid_simple-_North_America.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Electricity_grid_simple-_North_America.svg)

Diagram of an electrical grid (generation system in red, transmission system in blue, distribution system in green)

An **electrical grid** (or **electricity network**) is an interconnected network for [electricity delivery](/wiki/Electricity_delivery "Electricity delivery") from producers to consumers. Electrical grids consist of [power stations](/wiki/Power_station "Power station"), [electrical substations](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation") to step [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") up or down, [electric power transmission](/wiki/High_voltage_transmission_line "High voltage transmission line") to carry power over long distances, and finally [electric power distribution](/wiki/Electric_power_distribution "Electric power distribution") to customers. In that last step, voltage is stepped down again to the required service voltage. Power stations are typically built close to energy sources and far from densely populated areas. Electrical grids vary in size and can cover whole countries or continents. From small to large there are [microgrids](/wiki/Microgrid "Microgrid"), [wide area synchronous grids](/wiki/Wide_area_synchronous_grid "Wide area synchronous grid"), and [super grids](/wiki/Super_grid "Super grid"). The combined transmission and distribution network is part of electricity delivery, known as the *power grid*.

Grids are nearly always synchronous, meaning all distribution areas operate with [three phase](/wiki/Three_phase "Three phase") [alternating current](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current") (AC) frequencies synchronized (so that voltage swings occur at almost the same time). This allows transmission of AC power throughout the area, connecting the electricity generators with consumers. Grids can enable more efficient [electricity markets](/wiki/Electricity_market "Electricity market").

Although electrical grids are widespread, as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit), 1.4 billion people worldwide were not connected to an electricity grid.[[1]](#cite_note-1) As [electrification](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification") increases, the number of people with access to grid electricity is growing. About 840 million people (mostly in Africa), which is ca. 11% of the World's population, had no access to grid electricity in 2017, down from 1.2 billion in 2010.[[2]](#cite_note-Odarno_2019_j852-2)

Electrical grids can be prone to malicious intrusion or attack; thus, there is a need for [electric grid security](/wiki/Electric_grid_security "Electric grid security"). Also as electric grids modernize and introduce computer technology, cyber threats start to become a security risk.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Particular concerns relate to the more complex computer systems needed to manage grids.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Microgrid")]

Main article: [Microgrid](/wiki/Microgrid "Microgrid")

A microgrid is a local grid that is usually part of the regional wide-area synchronous grid, but which can disconnect and operate autonomously.[[5]](#cite_note-microgrids-5) It might do this in times when the main grid is affected by outages. This is known as [islanding](/wiki/Islanding "Islanding"), and it might run indefinitely on its own resources.

Compared to larger grids, microgrids typically use a lower voltage distribution network and distributed generators.[[6]](#cite_note-academia-6) Microgrids may not only be more resilient, but may be cheaper to implement in isolated areas.

A design goal is that a local area produces all of the energy it uses.[[5]](#cite_note-microgrids-5)

### Wide area synchronous grid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wide area synchronous grid")]

Main article: [Wide area synchronous grid](/wiki/Wide_area_synchronous_grid "Wide area synchronous grid")

A *wide area synchronous grid* (also called an "interconnection" in North America) is an electrical grid at a regional scale or greater that operates at a synchronized frequency and is electrically tied together during normal system conditions. For example, there are four major interconnections in North America (the [Western Interconnection](/wiki/Western_Interconnection "Western Interconnection"), the [Eastern Interconnection](/wiki/Eastern_Interconnection "Eastern Interconnection"), the [Quebec Interconnection](/wiki/Quebec_Interconnection "Quebec Interconnection") and the [Texas Interconnection](/wiki/Texas_Interconnection "Texas Interconnection")). In Europe, [one large grid connects most of Western Europe](/wiki/Synchronous_grid_of_Continental_Europe "Synchronous grid of Continental Europe"). These are also known as synchronous zones, the largest of which is the [synchronous grid of Continental Europe](/wiki/Synchronous_grid_of_Continental_Europe "Synchronous grid of Continental Europe") (ENTSO-E) with 667 [gigawatts](/wiki/Gigawatts "Gigawatts") (GW) of generation, and the widest region served being that of the [IPS/UPS](/wiki/IPS/UPS "IPS/UPS") system serving countries of the former Soviet Union. Synchronous grids with ample capacity facilitate [electricity market](/wiki/Electricity_market "Electricity market") trading across wide areas. In the ENTSO-E in 2008, over 350,000 megawatt hours were sold per day on the [European Energy Exchange](/wiki/European_Energy_Exchange "European Energy Exchange") (EEX).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Each of the interconnects in North America are run at a nominal 60 Hz, while those of Europe run at 50 Hz. Neighbouring interconnections with the same frequency and standards can be synchronized and directly connected to form a larger interconnection, or they may share power without synchronization via high-voltage direct current [power transmission lines](/wiki/Power_transmission_line "Power transmission line") ([DC ties](/wiki/DC_tie "DC tie")), or with variable-frequency transformers (VFTs), which permit a controlled flow of energy while also functionally isolating the independent AC frequencies of each side.

The benefits of synchronous zones include pooling of generation, resulting in lower generation costs; pooling of load, resulting in significant equalizing effects; common provisioning of reserves, resulting in cheaper primary and secondary reserve power costs; opening of the market, resulting in possibility of long-term contracts and short term power exchanges; and mutual assistance in the event of disturbances.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

One disadvantage of a wide-area synchronous grid is that problems in one part can have repercussions across the whole grid. For example, in 2018, [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") used more power than it generated due to a dispute with [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), leading to the phase across the whole [synchronous grid of Continental Europe](/wiki/Synchronous_grid_of_Continental_Europe "Synchronous grid of Continental Europe") lagging behind what it should have been. The frequency dropped to 49.996 Hz. This caused certain kinds of [clocks](/wiki/Clock "Clock") to become six minutes slow.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Super grid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Super grid")]

Main article: [Super grid](/wiki/Super_grid "Super grid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/TREC-Map-en.jpg/250px-TREC-Map-en.jpg)](/wiki/File:TREC-Map-en.jpg)

One conceptual plan of a super grid linking renewable sources across North Africa, the Middle East and Europe ([DESERTEC](/wiki/DESERTEC "DESERTEC"))[[10]](#cite_note-10)

A *super grid* or *supergrid* is a wide-area transmission network that is intended to make possible the trade of high volumes of electricity across great distances. It is sometimes also referred to as a *mega grid*. Super grids can support a global [energy transition](/wiki/Energy_transition "Energy transition") by smoothing local fluctuations of [wind energy](/wiki/Wind_energy "Wind energy") and [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_energy "Solar energy"). In this context, they are considered as a key technology to [mitigate](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"). Super grids typically use [high-voltage direct current](/wiki/High-voltage_direct_current "High-voltage direct current") (HVDC) to transmit electricity long distances. The latest generation of HVDC power lines can transmit energy with losses of only 1.6% per 1000 km.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Electric utilities between regions are many times interconnected for improved economy and reliability. [Electrical interconnectors](/wiki/Electrical_interconnector "Electrical interconnector") allow for economies of scale, allowing energy to be purchased from large, efficient sources. Utilities can draw power from generator reserves from a different region to ensure continuing, reliable power and diversify their loads. Interconnection also allows regions to have access to cheap bulk energy by receiving power from different sources. For example, one region may be producing cheap hydro power during high water seasons, but in low water seasons, another area may be producing cheaper power through wind, allowing both regions to access cheaper energy sources from one another during different times of the year. Neighboring utilities also help others to maintain the overall system frequency and also help manage tie transfers between utility regions.[[12]](#cite_note-History_of_Electric_Power_Systems-12)

Electricity Interconnection Level (EIL) of a grid is the ratio of the total interconnector power to the grid divided by the installed production capacity of the grid. Within the EU, it has set a target of national grids reaching 10% by 2020, and 15% by 2030.[[13]](#cite_note-auto-13)

## Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Components")]

### Generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Generation")]

Main article: [Electricity generation](/wiki/Electricity_generation "Electricity generation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Turbogenerator01.jpg/330px-Turbogenerator01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turbogenerator01.jpg)

[Turbo generator](/wiki/Turbo_generator "Turbo generator")

Electricity generation is the process of generating [electric power](/wiki/Electric_power "Electric power") at [power stations](/wiki/Power_station "Power station"). This is done ultimately from sources of [primary energy](/wiki/Primary_energy "Primary energy") typically with [electromechanical](/wiki/Electromechanical "Electromechanical") [generators](/wiki/Electric_generator "Electric generator") driven by [heat engines](/wiki/Heat_engine "Heat engine") from [fossil](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"), [nuclear](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power"), and [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") sources, or driven by the [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") of water or wind. Other power sources are [photovoltaics](/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") driven by solar insolation, and [grid batteries](/wiki/Grid_battery "Grid battery").[[nb 1]](#cite_note-14)

The sum of the power outputs of generators on the grid is the production of the grid, typically measured in [gigawatts](/wiki/Gigawatts "Gigawatts") (GW).

### Transmission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Transmission")]

Main article: [Electric power transmission](/wiki/Electric_power_transmission "Electric power transmission")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/500kV_3-Phase_Transmission_Lines.png/250px-500kV_3-Phase_Transmission_Lines.png)](/wiki/File:500kV_3-Phase_Transmission_Lines.png)

500 kV [three-phase electric power](/wiki/Three-phase_electric_power "Three-phase electric power") Transmission Lines at [Grand Coulee Dam](/wiki/Grand_Coulee_Dam "Grand Coulee Dam"); four circuits are shown; two additional circuits are obscured by trees on the right; the entire 7079 MW generation capacity of the dam is accommodated by these six circuits.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/A_network_diagram_of_the_%27nesta_case1354_pegase_high_voltage%27_electrical_power_system.png/250px-A_network_diagram_of_the_%27nesta_case1354_pegase_high_voltage%27_electrical_power_system.png)](/wiki/File:A_network_diagram_of_the_%27nesta_case1354_pegase_high_voltage%27_electrical_power_system.png)

Network diagram of a high voltage transmission system, showing the interconnection between the different voltage levels. This diagram depicts the electrical structure[[14]](#cite_note-CuffeKeane2017-15) of the network, rather than its physical geography.

Electric power transmission is the bulk movement of [electrical energy](/wiki/Electrical_energy "Electrical energy") from a generating site, via a web of interconnected lines, to an [electrical substation](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation"), from which is connected to the distribution system. This networked system of connections is distinct from the local wiring between high-voltage substations and customers. Transmission networks are built with redundant pathways to prevent a [single point of failure](/wiki/Single_point_of_failure "Single point of failure"). In case of line failures this [redundancy](/wiki/Redundancy_(engineering) "Redundancy (engineering)") allows power to be simply rerouted while repairs are done.

Because the power is often generated far from where it is consumed, the transmission system can cover great distances. For a given amount of power, transmission efficiency is greater at higher voltages and lower currents. Therefore, voltages are stepped up at the generating station, and stepped down at local substations for distribution to customers.

Most transmission is [three-phase](/wiki/Three-phase_electric_power "Three-phase electric power"). Three-phase, compared to single-phase, can deliver much more power for a given amount of wire, since the neutral and ground wires are shared.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Further, three-phase generators and motors are more efficient than their single-phase counterparts.

However, for conventional conductors one of the main losses are resistive losses which are a square law on current, and depend on distance. High voltage AC transmission lines can lose 1–4% per hundred miles.[[16]](#cite_note-17) However, [high-voltage direct current](/wiki/High-voltage_direct_current "High-voltage direct current") can have half the losses of AC. Over very long distances, these efficiencies can offset the additional cost of the required AC/DC converter stations at each end.

### Substations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Substations")]

Main article: [Electrical substation](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation")

Substations may perform many different functions but usually transform voltage from low to high (step up) and from high to low (step down). Between the generator and the final consumer, the voltage may be transformed several times.[[17]](#cite_note-eep-18)

The three main types of substations, by function, are:[[18]](#cite_note-UofC-19)

* Step-up substation: these use [transformers](/wiki/Transformer "Transformer") to raise the voltage coming from the generators and power plants so that power can be transmitted long distances more efficiently, with smaller currents.
* Step-down substation: these transformers lower the voltage coming from the transmission lines which can be used in industry or sent to a distribution substation.
* Distribution substation: these transform the voltage lower again for the distribution to end users.

Aside from transformers, other major components or functions of substations include:

* [Circuit breakers](/wiki/Circuit_breaker "Circuit breaker"): used to automatically break a circuit and isolate a fault in the system.[[19]](#cite_note-hayes-20)
* [Switches](/wiki/Switch "Switch"): to control the flow of electricity, and isolate equipment.[[20]](#cite_note-grady-21)
* The substation [busbar](/wiki/Busbar "Busbar"): typically a set of three conductors, one for each phase of current. The substation is organized around the buses, and they are connected to incoming lines, transformers, protection equipment, switches, and the outgoing lines.[[19]](#cite_note-hayes-20)
* [Lightning arresters](/wiki/Lightning_arrester "Lightning arrester")
* [Capacitors](/wiki/Capacitor "Capacitor") for [power factor](/wiki/Power_factor "Power factor") correction
* [Synchronous condensers](/wiki/Synchronous_condenser "Synchronous condenser") for power factor correction and grid stability

### Electric power distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Electric power distribution")]

Main article: [Electric power distribution](/wiki/Electric_power_distribution "Electric power distribution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Electricity_Grid_Schematic_English.svg/500px-Electricity_Grid_Schematic_English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Electricity_Grid_Schematic_English.svg)

General layout of electricity grids. Voltages and depictions of electrical lines are typical for Germany and other European systems.

Distribution is the final stage in the delivery of power; it carries electricity from the transmission system to individual consumers. Substations connect to the transmission system and lower the transmission voltage to medium voltage ranging between 2 [kV](/wiki/Volt "Volt") and 35 kV. But the voltage levels varies very much between different countries, in Sweden medium voltage are normally 10 [kV](/wiki/Volt "Volt") between 20 kV.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Primary distribution lines carry this medium voltage power to [distribution transformers](/wiki/Distribution_transformer "Distribution transformer") located near the customer's premises. Distribution transformers again lower the voltage to the [utilization voltage](/wiki/Utilization_voltage "Utilization voltage"). Customers demanding a much larger amount of power may be connected directly to the primary distribution level or the [subtransmission](/wiki/Subtransmission "Subtransmission") level.[[22]](#cite_note-HSW-23)

Distribution networks are divided into two types, radial or network.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

In cities and towns of North America, the grid tends to follow the classic *radially fed* design. A substation receives its power from the transmission network, the power is stepped down with a transformer and sent to a [bus](/wiki/Busbar "Busbar") from which feeders fan out in all directions across the countryside. These feeders carry three-phase power, and tend to follow the major streets near the substation. As the distance from the substation grows, the fanout continues as smaller laterals spread out to cover areas missed by the feeders. This tree-like structure grows outward from the substation, but for reliability reasons, usually contains at least one unused backup connection to a nearby substation. This connection can be enabled in case of an emergency, so that a portion of a substation's service territory can be alternatively fed by another substation.

### Storage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Storage")]

Main article: [Grid energy storage](/wiki/Grid_energy_storage "Grid energy storage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/20241206_Grid_energy_storage.svg/250px-20241206_Grid_energy_storage.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20241206_Grid_energy_storage.svg)

Energy from fossil or nuclear power plants and renewable sources is stored for use by customers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/20241201_Power_grid_storage_energy_flow_-_daily.svg/250px-20241201_Power_grid_storage_energy_flow_-_daily.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20241201_Power_grid_storage_energy_flow_-_daily.svg)

Simplified grid energy flow over the course of a day

*Grid energy storage* (also called *large-scale energy storage*) is a collection of methods used for [energy storage](/wiki/Energy_storage "Energy storage") on a large scale within an [electrical power grid](/wiki/Grid_(electricity) "Grid (electricity)"). Electrical energy is stored during times when electricity is plentiful and inexpensive (especially from [intermittent power](/wiki/Variable_renewable_energy "Variable renewable energy") sources such as [renewable electricity](/wiki/Renewable_electricity "Renewable electricity") from [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power"), [tidal power](/wiki/Tidal_power "Tidal power") and [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power")) or when demand is low, and later power is generated when demand is high, and electricity prices tend to be higher.

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit), the largest form of grid energy storage is dammed [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"), with both conventional hydroelectric generation as well as [pumped storage hydroelectricity](/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity "Pumped-storage hydroelectricity").

Developments in battery storage have enabled commercially viable projects to store energy during peak production and release during peak demand, and for use when production unexpectedly falls giving time for slower responding resources to be brought online.

Two alternatives to grid storage are the use of [peaking power plants](/wiki/Peaking_power_plant "Peaking power plant") to fill in supply gaps and [demand response](/wiki/Demand_response "Demand response") to shift load to other times.

## Functionalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Functionalities")]

### Demand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Demand")]

The demand, or load on an electrical grid is the total electrical power being removed by the users of the grid.

The graph of the demand over time is called the *demand curve*.

[Baseload](/wiki/Baseload "Baseload") is the minimum load on the grid over any given period, [peak demand](/wiki/Peak_demand "Peak demand") is the maximum load. Historically, baseload was commonly met by equipment that was relatively cheap to run, that ran continuously for weeks or months at a time, but globally this is becoming less common. The extra peak demand requirements are sometimes produced by expensive [peaking plants](/wiki/Peaking_plant "Peaking plant") that are generators optimised to come on-line quickly but these too are becoming less common.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

However, if the demand of electricity exceeds the capacity of a local power grid, it will cause safety issues like burning out.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### Voltage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Voltage")]

Grids are designed to supply electricity to their customers at largely constant voltages. This has to be achieved with varying demand, variable [reactive](/wiki/Reactive_power "Reactive power") loads, and even nonlinear loads, with electricity provided by generators and distribution and transmission equipment that are not perfectly reliable.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Often grids use [tap changers](/wiki/Tap_changer "Tap changer") on transformers near to the consumers to adjust the voltage and keep it within specification.

### Frequency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Frequency")]

Main article: [Utility frequency](/wiki/Utility_frequency "Utility frequency")

In a synchronous grid all the generators must run at the same frequency, and must stay very nearly in phase with each other and the grid. Generation and consumption must be balanced across the entire grid, because energy is consumed as it is produced. For rotating generators, a local [governor](/wiki/Governor_(device) "Governor (device)") regulates the driving torque, maintaining almost constant rotation speed as loading changes. Energy is stored in the immediate short term by the rotational kinetic energy of the generators.

Although the speed is kept largely constant, small deviations from the nominal system frequency are very important in regulating individual generators and are used as a way of assessing the equilibrium of the grid as a whole. When the grid is lightly loaded the grid frequency runs above the nominal frequency, and this is taken as an indication by [Automatic generation control](/wiki/Automatic_generation_control "Automatic generation control") (AGC) systems across the network that generators should reduce their output. Conversely, when the grid is heavily loaded, the frequency naturally slows, and governors adjust their generators so that more power is output ([droop speed control](/wiki/Droop_speed_control "Droop speed control")). When generators have identical droop speed control settings it ensures that multiple parallel generators with the same settings share load in proportion to their rating.

In addition, there's often central control, which can change the parameters of the AGC systems over timescales of a minute or longer to further adjust the regional network flows and the operating frequency of the grid.

For timekeeping purposes, the nominal frequency will be allowed to vary in the short term, but is adjusted to prevent [line-operated clocks](/wiki/Electric_clock "Electric clock") from gaining or losing significant time over the course of a whole 24 hour period.

Neighbouring grids that are not directly connected are almost always out-of-phase with each other. Instead, [high-voltage direct current](/wiki/High-voltage_direct_current "High-voltage direct current") lines or [variable-frequency transformers](/wiki/Variable-frequency_transformer "Variable-frequency transformer") are used, which allow two out-of-phase synchronous grids to share power.

### Capacity and firm capacity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Capacity and firm capacity")]

The sum of the maximum power outputs ([nameplate capacity](/wiki/Nameplate_capacity "Nameplate capacity")) of the generators attached to an electrical grid might be considered to be the capacity of the grid.

However, in practice, they are never run flat out simultaneously. Typically, some generators are kept running at lower output powers ([spinning reserve](/wiki/Spinning_reserve "Spinning reserve")) to deal with failures as well as variation in demand. In addition generators can be off-line for maintenance or other reasons, such as availability of energy inputs (fuel, water, wind, sun etc.) or pollution constraints.

**Firm capacity** is the maximum power output on a grid that is immediately available over a given time period, and is a far more useful figure.

### Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Production")]

Most grid codes specify that the load is shared between the generators in [merit order](/wiki/Merit_order "Merit order") according to their [marginal cost](/wiki/Marginal_cost "Marginal cost") (i.e. cheapest first) and sometimes their environmental impact. Thus cheap electricity providers tend to be run flat out almost all the time, and the more expensive producers are only run when necessary.

## Management, operation and ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Management, operation and ownership")]

A central authority is usually designated to facilitate communication and develop protocols to maintain a stable grid. For example, the [North American Electric Reliability Corporation](/wiki/North_American_Electric_Reliability_Corporation "North American Electric Reliability Corporation") gained binding powers in the United States in 2006, and has advisory powers in the applicable parts of Canada and Mexico. The U.S. government has also designated [National Interest Electric Transmission Corridors](/wiki/National_Interest_Electric_Transmission_Corridor "National Interest Electric Transmission Corridor"), where it believes transmission bottlenecks have developed.

Where multiple utilities own generators connected to one electrical grid [power pools](/wiki/Power_pool "Power pool") are often formed which have agreements about responsibilities.

Each grid may have a system operator that is responsible for day-to-day running of one or more grids.

## Failures and issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Failures and issues")]

Failures are usually associated with generators or power transmission lines tripping circuit breakers due to faults leading to a loss of generation capacity for customers, or excess demand. This will often cause the frequency to reduce, and the remaining generators will react and together attempt to stabilize above the minimum. If that is not possible then a number of scenarios can occur.

A large failure in one part of the grid—unless quickly compensated for—can cause current to re-route itself to flow from the remaining generators to consumers over transmission lines of insufficient capacity, causing further failures. One downside to a widely connected grid is thus the possibility of [cascading failure](/wiki/Cascading_failure "Cascading failure") and widespread [power outage](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage").

### Brownout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Brownout")]

Main article: [Brownout (electricity)](/wiki/Brownout_(electricity) "Brownout (electricity)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/20110313-TokyoTower.jpg/250px-20110313-TokyoTower.jpg)](/wiki/File:20110313-TokyoTower.jpg)

A brownout near [Tokyo Tower](/wiki/Tokyo_Tower "Tokyo Tower") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan

A *brownout* is an intentional or unintentional drop in voltage in an electrical [power supply](/wiki/Power_supply "Power supply") system. Intentional brownouts are used for load reduction in an emergency.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The reduction lasts for minutes or hours, as opposed to short-term [voltage sag](/wiki/Voltage_sag "Voltage sag") (or dip). The term brownout comes from the dimming experienced by incandescent lighting when the voltage sags. A [voltage reduction](/wiki/Voltage_reduction "Voltage reduction") may be an effect of disruption of an electrical grid, or may occasionally be imposed in an effort to reduce load and prevent a [power outage](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage"), known as a [blackout](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

### Blackout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Blackout")]

Main article: [Power outage](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage")

A *power outage* (also called a *power cut*, a *power out*, a *power blackout*, *power failure* or a *blackout*) is a loss of the electric power to a particular area.

Power failures can be caused by faults at power stations, damage to electric transmission lines, [substations](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation") or other parts of the [distribution](/wiki/Electricity_distribution "Electricity distribution") system, a [short circuit](/wiki/Short_circuit "Short circuit"), [cascading failure](/wiki/Cascading_failure#Cascading_failure_in_power_transmission "Cascading failure"), [fuse](/wiki/Fuse_(electrical) "Fuse (electrical)") or [circuit breaker](/wiki/Circuit_breaker "Circuit breaker") operation, and human error.

Power failures are particularly critical at sites where the environment and public safety are at risk. Institutions such as [hospitals](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital"), [sewage treatment](/wiki/Sewage_treatment "Sewage treatment") plants, [mines](/wiki/Mining "Mining"), shelters and the like will usually have backup power sources such as [standby generators](/wiki/Emergency_power_system "Emergency power system"), which will automatically start up when electrical power is lost. Other critical systems, such as [telecommunication](/wiki/Telecommunication "Telecommunication"), are also required to have emergency power. The [battery room](/wiki/Battery_room "Battery room") of a telephone exchange usually has arrays of [lead–acid batteries](/wiki/Lead%E2%80%93acid_battery "Lead–acid battery") for backup and also a socket for connecting a generator during extended periods of outage.

### Load shedding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Load shedding")]

Main article: [Demand response](/wiki/Demand_response "Demand response")

[Electrical generation](/wiki/Electrical_generation "Electrical generation") and transmission systems may not always meet peak demand requirements— the greatest amount of [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") required by all utility customers within a given region. In these situations, overall demand must be lowered, either by turning off service to some devices or cutting back the supply voltage ([brownouts](/wiki/Brownout_(electricity) "Brownout (electricity)")), in order to prevent uncontrolled service disruptions such as power outages (widespread blackouts) or equipment damage. Utilities may impose load shedding on service areas via targeted blackouts, [rolling blackouts](/wiki/Rolling_blackout "Rolling blackout") or by agreements with specific high-use industrial consumers to turn off equipment at times of system-wide peak demand.

### Black start

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Black start")]

Main article: [Black start](/wiki/Black_start "Black start")

[![City skyline at dusk with only a very few office building windows lit](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Toronto_ON_2003_Blackout.jpg/250px-Toronto_ON_2003_Blackout.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_ON_2003_Blackout.jpg)

Toronto during the [Northeast blackout of 2003](/wiki/Northeast_blackout_of_2003 "Northeast blackout of 2003"), which required black-starting of generating stations

A *black start* is the process of restoring an electric power station or a part of an [electric grid](/wiki/Electric_grid "Electric grid") to operation without relying on the external [electric power transmission network](/wiki/Transmission_network "Transmission network") to recover from a total or partial shutdown.[[28]](#cite_note-Knight01-29)

Normally, the electric power used within the plant is provided from the station's own generators. If all of the plant's main generators are shut down, station service power is provided by drawing power from the grid through the plant's transmission line. However, during a wide-area outage, off-site power from the grid is not available. In the absence of grid power, a so-called black start needs to be performed to [bootstrap](/wiki/Bootstrapping#Electric_power_grid "Bootstrapping") the power grid into operation.

To provide a black start, some power stations have small [diesel generators](/wiki/Diesel_generator "Diesel generator"), normally called the *black start diesel generator* (BSDG), which can be used to start larger generators (of several [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatt "Megawatt") capacity), which in turn can be used to start the main power station generators. Generating plants using steam turbines require station service power of up to 10% of their capacity for [boiler feedwater pumps](/wiki/Boiler_feedwater_pump "Boiler feedwater pump"), boiler forced-draft combustion air blowers, and for fuel preparation. It is uneconomical to provide such a large standby capacity at each station, so black-start power must be provided over designated tie lines from another station. Often hydroelectric power plants are designated as the black-start sources to restore network interconnections. A hydroelectric station needs very little initial power to start (just enough to open the intake gates and provide [excitation](/wiki/Excitation_(magnetic) "Excitation (magnetic)") current to the generator field coils), and can put a large block of power on line very quickly to allow start-up of fossil-fuel or nuclear stations. Certain types of [combustion turbine](/wiki/Combustion_turbine "Combustion turbine") can be configured for black start, providing another option in places without suitable hydroelectric plants.[[29]](#cite_note-30) In 2017 a utility in Southern California has successfully demonstrated the use of a battery energy storage system to provide a black start, firing up a combined cycle gas turbine from an idle state.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### Obsolescence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Obsolescence")]

Despite novel institutional arrangements and network designs, power delivery infrastructures is experiencing aging across the developed world. Contributing factors include:

* Aging equipment – older equipment has higher [failure rates](/wiki/Failure_rate "Failure rate"), leading to customer interruption rates affecting the economy and society; also, older assets and facilities lead to higher inspection [maintenance](/wiki/Maintenance,_repair,_and_operations "Maintenance, repair, and operations") costs and further [repair](/wiki/Maintenance,_repair,_and_operations "Maintenance, repair, and operations") and [restoration](/wiki/Renovation "Renovation") costs.
* Obsolete system layout – older areas require serious additional substation sites and [rights-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)") that cannot be obtained in the current area and are forced to use existing, insufficient facilities.
* Outdated engineering – traditional tools for [power delivery](/wiki/Power_delivery "Power delivery") planning and engineering are ineffective in addressing current problems of aged equipment, obsolete system layouts, and modern deregulated loading levels.
* Old cultural value – [planning](/wiki/Planning "Planning"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), operating of system using concepts and procedures that worked in vertically integrated industry exacerbate the problem under a deregulated industry.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## Trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Trends")]

### Demand response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Demand response")]

[Demand response](/wiki/Demand_response "Demand response") is a grid management technique where retail or wholesale customers are requested or incentivised either electronically or manually to reduce their load. Currently, transmission grid operators use demand response to request load reduction from major energy users such as industrial plants.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Technologies such as smart metering can encourage customers to use power when electricity is plentiful by allowing for variable pricing.

### Smart grid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Smart grid")]

This section is an excerpt from [Smart grid](/wiki/Smart_grid "Smart grid").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smart_grid&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Staying_big_or_getting_smaller.jpg/500px-Staying_big_or_getting_smaller.jpg)](/wiki/File:Staying_big_or_getting_smaller.jpg)

Characteristics of a traditional centralized electrical system (left) vis-à-vis those of a smart grid (right)

The [smart grid](/wiki/Smart_grid "Smart grid") is an enhancement of the 20th century electrical grid, using two-way communications and distributed so-called intelligent devices.[[33]](#cite_note-Smart_grid_tsg-34) Two-way flows of electricity and information could improve the delivery network. Research is mainly focused on three systems of a smart grid – the infrastructure system, the management system, and the protection system.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Electronic power conditioning and control of the production and distribution of electricity are important aspects of the smart grid.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

The smart grid represents the full suite of current and proposed responses to the challenges of electricity supply. Numerous contributions to the overall improvement of energy infrastructure efficiency are anticipated from the deployment of smart grid technology, in particular including [demand-side management](/wiki/Demand_side_management "Demand side management"). The improved flexibility of the smart grid permits greater penetration of highly variable renewable energy sources such as [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") and [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power"), even without the addition of [energy storage](/wiki/Energy_storage "Energy storage"). Smart grids could also monitor/control residential devices that are noncritical during periods of peak power consumption, and return their function during nonpeak hours.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

A smart grid includes a variety of operation and energy measures:

* [Advanced metering infrastructure](/wiki/Advanced_metering_infrastructure "Advanced metering infrastructure") (of which [smart meters](/wiki/Smart_meter "Smart meter") are a generic name for any utility side device even if it is more capable e.g. a fiber optic router)
* Smart [distribution boards](/wiki/Distribution_board "Distribution board") and [circuit breakers](/wiki/Circuit_breaker "Circuit breaker") integrated with [home control](/wiki/Home_control "Home control") and [demand response](/wiki/Demand_response "Demand response") (*behind the meter* from a utility perspective)
  + [Load control switches](/wiki/Load_control_switch "Load control switch") and [smart appliances](/wiki/Smart_appliance "Smart appliance"), often financed by efficiency gains on municipal programs (e.g. [PACE financing](/wiki/PACE_financing "PACE financing"))
* [Renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") resources, including the capacity to charge parked ([electric vehicle](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle")) batteries or larger arrays of batteries recycled from these, or other [energy storage](/wiki/Energy_storage "Energy storage").
* Energy efficient resources
* Electric surplus distribution by power lines and auto-smart switch
* Sufficient utility grade fiber [broadband](/wiki/Broadband "Broadband") to connect and monitor the above, with wireless as a backup. Sufficient spare if "dark" capacity to ensure failover, often leased for revenue.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

Concerns with smart grid technology mostly focus on smart meters, items enabled by them, and general security issues. Roll-out of smart grid technology also implies a fundamental re-engineering of the electricity services industry, although typical usage of the term is focused on the technical infrastructure.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Smart grid policy is organized in Europe as Smart Grid [European Technology Platform](/wiki/European_Technology_Platform "European Technology Platform").[[40]](#cite_note-41) Policy in the United States is described in [Title 42 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_42_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 42 of the United States Code").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Smart grids are not to be confused with [sector coupled smart energy systems](/wiki/Sector_coupling "Sector coupling"), as smart grids primarily refers to the power sector, while smart energy system use an integrated holistic focus which also includes other sectors of the energy system with the intent to create synergies between them.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

### Grid defection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Grid defection")]

Resistance to distributed generation among grid operators may encourage providers to leave the grid and instead distribute power to smaller geographies.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)

The [Rocky Mountain Institute](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_Institute "Rocky Mountain Institute")[[46]](#cite_note-47) and other studies[[47]](#cite_note-48) foresee widescale grid defection. However, grid defection may be less likely in places such as Germany that have greater power demands in the winter.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Electrical_grid&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: History")]

Early electric energy was produced near the device or service requiring that energy. In the 1880s, electricity competed with steam, hydraulics, and especially [coal gas](/wiki/Coal_gas "Coal gas"). Coal gas was first produced on customer's premises but later evolved into [gasification](/wiki/Gasification "Gasification") plants that enjoyed [economies of scale](/wiki/Economies_of_scale "Economies of scale"). In the industrialized world, cities had networks of piped gas, used for lighting. But gas lamps produced poor light, wasted heat, made rooms hot and smoky, and gave off [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"). They also posed a fire hazard. In the 1880s electric lighting soon became advantageous compared to gas lighting.

[Electric utility](/wiki/Electric_utility "Electric utility") companies established [central stations](/wiki/Central_station_(electricity) "Central station (electricity)") to take advantage of economies of scale and moved to centralized power generation, distribution, and system management.[[49]](#cite_note-Distributed_Generation-50) After the [war of the currents](/wiki/War_of_the_currents "War of the currents") was settled in favor of [AC power](/wiki/AC_power "AC power"), with long-distance power transmission it became possible to interconnect stations to balance the loads and improve load factors. Historically, transmission and distribution lines were owned by the same company, but starting in the 1990s, many countries have [liberalized](/wiki/Electricity_liberalization "Electricity liberalization") the regulation of the [electricity market](/wiki/Electricity_market "Electricity market") in ways that have led to the separation of the electricity transmission business from the distribution business.[[50]](#cite_note-Pacific_Northwest_National_Laboratory_report-51)

In the United Kingdom, [Charles Merz](/wiki/Charles_Merz "Charles Merz"), of the [Merz & McLellan](/wiki/Merz_%26_McLellan "Merz & McLellan") consulting partnership, built the [Neptune Bank Power Station](/wiki/Neptune_Bank_Power_Station "Neptune Bank Power Station") near [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") in 1901,[[51]](#cite_note-52) and by 1912 had developed into the largest integrated power system in Europe.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Merz was appointed head of a parliamentary committee and his findings led to the Williamson Report of 1918, which in turn created the [Electricity (Supply) Act 1919](/wiki/Electricity_(Supply)_Act_1919 "Electricity (Supply) Act 1919"). The bill was the first step towards an integrated electricity system. In 1925 the [Weir](/wiki/William_Weir,_1st_Viscount_Weir "William Weir, 1st Viscount Weir") Committee recommended the creation of a "national gridiron" and so the [Electricity (Supply) Act 1926](/wiki/Electricity_(Supply)_Act_1926 "Electricity (Supply) Act 1926") created the [Central Electricity Board](/wiki/Central_Electricity_Board "Central Electricity Board") (CEB).[[53]](#cite_note-54) The CEB standardized the nation's electricity supply and established the first synchronized AC grid, running at 132 kilovolts and 50 [hertz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz") but initially operated as regional grids. After brief overnight interconnection in 1937 they permanently and officially joined in 1938 becoming the [UK National Grid](/wiki/National_Grid_(UK) "National Grid (UK)").

In France, [electrification](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification") began in the 1900s, with 700 [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_France "Communes of France") in 1919, and 36,528 in 1938. At the same time, these close networks began to interconnect: Paris in 1907 at 12 kV, the Pyrénées in 1923 at 150 kV, and finally almost all of the country interconnected by 1938 at 220 kV. In 1946, the grid was the world's most dense. That year the state nationalised the industry, by uniting the private companies as [Électricité de France](/wiki/%C3%89lectricit%C3%A9_de_France "Électricité de France"). The frequency was standardised at 50 Hz, and the 225 kV network replaced 110 kV and 120 kV. Since 1956, service voltage has been standardised at 220/380 V, replacing the previous 127/220 V. During the 1970s, the 400 kV network, the new European standard, was implemented. Starting on May 29, 1986, the end user service voltage will progressively change to 230/400 V ±10%.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In the United States in the 1920s, utilities formed joint-operations to share peak load coverage and backup power. In 1934, with the passage of the [Public Utility Holding Company Act](/wiki/Public_Utility_Holding_Company_Act "Public Utility Holding Company Act") (U.S.), electric utilities were recognized as [public goods](/wiki/Public_good_(economics) "Public good (economics)") of importance and were given outlined restrictions and regulatory oversight of their operations. The [Energy Policy Act of 1992](/wiki/Energy_Policy_Act_of_1992 "Energy Policy Act of 1992") required transmission line owners to allow electric generation companies open access to their network[[49]](#cite_note-Distributed_Generation-50)[[56]](#cite_note-Electric_Power_Planning-57) and led to a restructuring of how the electric industry operated in an effort to create competition in power generation. No longer were electric utilities built as vertical monopolies, where generation, transmission and distribution were handled by a single company. Now, the three stages could be split among various companies, in an effort to provide fair access to high voltage transmission.[[12]](#cite_note-History_of_Electric_Power_Systems-12)[[13]](#cite_note-auto-13) The [Energy Policy Act of 2005](/wiki/Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005 "Energy Policy Act of 2005") allowed incentives and loan guarantees for alternative energy production and advance innovative technologies that avoided [greenhouse emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions").

In China, electrification began in the 1950s.[[57]](#cite_note-58) In August 1961, the electrification of the Baoji-Fengzhou section of the [Baocheng Railway](/wiki/Baocheng_Railway "Baocheng Railway") was completed and delivered for operation, becoming China's first [electrified railway](/wiki/Electrified_railway "Electrified railway").[[58]](#cite_note-59) From 1958 to 1998, China's electrified railway reached 6,200 miles (10,000 kilometres).[[59]](#cite_note-60) As of the end of 2017, this number has reached 54,000 miles (87,000 kilometres).[[60]](#cite_note-61) In the current [railway electrification system](/wiki/Railway_electrification_system "Railway electrification system") of China, [State Grid Corporation of China](https://g.esgcc.com.cn/)—[Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20211221190902/https://g.esgcc.com.cn/) 2021-12-21 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")—is an important power supplier. In 2019, it completed the power supply project of China's important electrified railways in its operating areas, such as [Jingtong Railway](/wiki/Jingtong_Railway "Jingtong Railway"), [Haoji Railway](/wiki/Haoji_Railway "Haoji Railway"), [Zhengzhou–Wanzhou high-speed railway](/wiki/Zhengzhou%E2%80%93Wanzhou_high-speed_railway "Zhengzhou–Wanzhou high-speed railway"), et cetera, providing power supply guarantee for 110 traction stations, and its cumulative power line construction length reached 6,586 kilometres.[[61]](#cite_note-62)
